name: B.S. in Concrete Industry Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This hybrid business-plus-concrete-technology program (the national CIM/NSMC model) sequences a clean concrete spine (CIM 101 -> 210 -> 215/310/315 -> 405 -> 410 Senior Project, plus CIM 497 Co-op) alongside a business/industrial-management block (ACCT 117/215, MGMT 390, MRKT 330, FIN 315, MNET 315/416/420, MIS 245) and supporting construction courses (CET 313/314/413/423). The two tracks interleave sensibly toward a management-of-the-concrete-supply-chain outcome.
dimensions.suggestion: Make the intended blend explicit by grouping the CIM technical courses and the MTSM business courses under labeled headings so students see the deliberate business/technology structure.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph (deepest chain 4) is shallow but appropriate for a management-oriented degree, and the concrete sequence is internally consistent (CIM 101 -> 210 -> 215/310/315; CIM 405 <- 205). The 4 dangling references (CHEM 125A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A) are a lab code and gen-ed placeholders, not real breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the CHEM 125A lab and GER/PHYS placeholder references, and confirm CIM 205 is a stated prerequisite for CIM 405 wherever the sequence assumes it.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data; only the plan of study and course descriptions appear. This is a real omission for a professionally aligned catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes spanning concrete technology, quality assurance, and business/management competencies expected of CIM graduates.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, delta 0.0) and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The split is transparent, though the technical concrete core (roughly eight CIM courses) is deliberately lighter than the large business/industrial-management block, which is by design for this degree but worth surfacing.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a credit-summary breakdown (concrete technology vs. business/management vs. gen-ed) so the intentional business weighting is transparent to prospective students.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and course descriptions are complete and specific, but the plan includes several unlabeled 'None' rows (electives/GER) and a missing CHEM 125A lab code, leaving a few requirement rows under-specified.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace blank 'None' rows with explicit named placeholders and restore the CHEM 125A lab entry.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program aligns well with concrete-industry practice: concrete properties and testing (CIM 205), advanced testing and QA (CIM 405), production/delivery management (CIM 310), quality systems and ISO (MNET 420), production scheduling (MNET 416), and a required co-op (CIM 497). The chief currency gaps are sustainability/low-carbon concrete and digital supply-chain automation.
dimensions.suggestion: Add sustainable/low-carbon concrete content (supplementary cementitious materials, carbon-cured and recycled-aggregate concrete) into CIM 205/405.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 32 sampled courses have descriptions, and the CIM entries are specific and applications-oriented (CIM 205 on how aggregates/cements/admixtures affect fresh and hardened concrete; CIM 310 on managing the order-and-delivery process; MNET 420 on TQM/SPC/ISO). Business-core descriptions (ACCT, FIN, MGMT) are equally clear.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit and internally consistent with grade conditions (ACCT 215 <- 'ACCT 115 or ACCT 117'; MRKT 330 <- 'MGMT 190 or MGMT 390 or departmental approval'; CIM 210 <- CIM 101). Only the gen-ed/lab placeholders are missing, and the shallow chain reflects the program's management orientation rather than a defect.

missingTopics.rationale: CIM 205/405 cover properties, testing and QA but not decarbonization, alternative cements, or life-cycle assessment, now a top industry priority.

missingTopics.topic: Digital batching, telematics and Industry 4.0 in ready-mix
missingTopics.rationale: CIM 310 Concrete Products and Delivery is traditional; automation, sensors and telematics in production and fleet management are absent.
CIM 310 addresses order/delivery but there is no data-driven logistics optimization or analytics course.

missingTopics.topic: Technical sales and CRM for construction materials
missingTopics.rationale: MRKT 330 is general marketing, yet CIM careers are sales-intensive; a technical-sales/CRM course tailored to the concrete industry would fit.
CIM 405 covers testing/QA but structural durability, forensic evaluation and repair of concrete are underrepresented.

emergingTrends.rationale: Owners and codes increasingly demand embodied-carbon and ESG data, which CIM 205/405 could incorporate.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital supply chain and fleet optimization
emergingTrends.rationale: Telematics and route/plant optimization are transforming ready-mix delivery, extending CIM 310.

emergingTrends.trend: Data analytics and AI for quality assurance
emergingTrends.rationale: CIM 405 QA is a natural place to add sensor-driven, predictive quality analytics.
Additive concrete methods are moving from research to practice and could feature in CIM 315.
Recycled aggregates and carbon-cured concrete tie sustainability to the CIM technical core.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes covering both concrete technology and business/management competencies.
Add sustainable / low-carbon concrete content and life-cycle assessment into CIM 205/405.
Introduce digital supply-chain and logistics analytics (extend CIM 310).
Add a technical-sales/CRM elective aligned to the concrete industry's sales-driven careers.
Resolve placeholder references (CHEM 125A, PHYS 102A, GER 200/300) and label blank plan rows.
